speaker: interviewee
text: This is the wedding of my sister, [POINTING] Malka, in September of 19-- in September of 1939. This is her-- this is her husband, Rolf. This is my cousin, Leib. This is his brother, Moshe. If you notice, the-- the yellow Stars of David, the-- the white armband with the yellow-- with the-- it's a navy blue Star of David, I think, inside. This is Elijah. And this is Mrs. Richter. She opened the garden for my sister's wedding. I have my mommy here. I have my daddy. And my-- these are the two girls, [? Haika ?] and [? Faiga ?]; [? Marmer ?], whose father came back from [INAUDIBLE] to tell us-- these two-- these two girls here-- whose father came back from [INAUDIBLE] to tell us that my brother had-- was killed, because he stole a piece of bread. All these people-- all these people almost perished in the Holocaust. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mommy was lighter, yeah. I was darker-skinned. This is my mommy; [POINTING] my little sister, Mesia. She was 10 years old here. And this is myself at 12 and 1/2; made in 19-- in September of 1939, at my sister's wedding, in the garden of Mr. and Mrs. Richter. My mommy and my sister perished in the Holocaust. This was made in Chrzanow, Poland. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is the wedding [POINTING] of my cousin, Rachel and [? Henriga ?] Wordsman. He died a few years ago. She's still living in Florida. This is my grandmother, my bubbe, Rosa. And this is the father of [? Hemia ?]. This is my daddy, and this is my mommy. This is my Aunt [? Scheindela ?] and her husband, Motel Frisch. This is my Aunt Golda. This is my Uncle [? Gabriel ?]. This is my cousin. This is my uncle, my father's brother. This is my little sister Mesia. See, she looks so much like her-- here too That's my cousin [? Rasia ?]. She married a Czech, and she lives in Czechoslovakia. She married him in the camp, in Theresienstadt. That's my cousin, Malka-- Mali. Most of them died in Auschwitz. They died. They were slaughtered. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This [POINTING] is my daddy, age 18, when he was in the army. In 1917, he fought in the Austrian-- in the Austrian army. And this is my uncle, Joseph, my father's brother. He also died in Auschwitz, as did my father. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] This is my-- [POINTING] my [NON-ENGLISH], my mommy's-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --father. He died after being attacked by Polish people with bayonets. They attacked them in his house and-- [PAUSES FOR 47 SECONDS] --a waste. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SIGHS] This is my sister and her husband, but here they were just engaged to be married. They married in 1939. And 1940, they had a baby. And they all went to Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was a beautiful, beautiful girl. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is the wedding of my husband and myself, in 1953, February 28. We have been married now for 42 years. And we are still together, thank God. [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] This is-- this is my son, Larry, the [POINTING] one who is here; my daughter-in-law, Hildy, my granddaughter, Lauren, my grandson, Adam, and my grandson, Jake. And I love them dearly. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I was in Florida last March, and it was March 1st that I had my speech, my lecture, in Duncan Middle School, where I was awarded the Certificate of Special Recognition by the principal, Mr. Don Rott, and Mrs. Loretta Eisele, educator. I'm very proud of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- this article was in the paper when I spoke to-- in Duncan Middle School, and it's an article written about me, the accomplishment that I do with the children, how they appreciate it, and how much they're learning. Here it shows how they greet me. After I'm done speaking, they come over and they kiss me. They greeted me with flowers. This is Loretta Eisele, [POINTING] my husband, myself; Loretta Eisele, Mr. Rott, and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here are the flowers and the children. You see how sad they are after they-- after they listen to me talk? And you see the flowers on the bottom there and-- and-- and here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm really very-- very-- we-- I feel very rewarded for my job. I do. I feel gratified, I must tell you. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: See how they cry? They're all there.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You could hear a pin drop when I speak. They are so quiet and so full of love-- boys and girls. They wait in line to come and kiss me and hug me. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] On April 23rd, 1995 I visited Flossenburg, the camp that I spoke about. And it was 50 years-- have passed. I was very emotional. I found the camp very much intact, and the German women living in some of the barracks, and the soil that is so saturated with Jewish blood. And by the way, not only Jewish people died there. Non-Jewish people died there, too. This is the-- the gestapo [POINTING] headquarters, just as it was when I remember it, during our death march. Here I recognized the commandant that-- that tortured me. I bought the pictures. This is inside the gate where I was stripped to nakedness and-- and beaten to death-- 1/2 dead. These are the crematoriums that people were-- you know. Before they were burned, they were taken into a room where they were stripped of the gold teeth and everything. And then they were burned in the crematoriums. This is my husband with this-- at the site of the crematoria. This is the watchtower. The Nazis would shoot. From these windows they would shoot the-- the prisoners in the appell place. This is the gate opening up to the gestapo headquarters. And these are monuments erected after the war for-- from-- for people who had been shot to death while fleeing the Nazis. It was a very emotional day for me, and I will never forget it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is again, April-- this is also March 1st, 1995, when I spoke in Miami Beach Gardens, Duncan Middle School. Again, I was greeted with-- this is Loretta Eisele, the Holocaust teacher, myself. [POINTING] I was greeted with flowers, and with banners, and with gifts from the children. Here I'm conducting my lecture. This is Mr. Rott, giving me the certificate of recognition, also flowers. These are children who wanted pictures with me. This is one of the girls that was photographed with me. And here you can see how they kiss me and they hug me after I'm finished talking. This is how grateful they are for my coming to them and-- and teaching them about the Holocaust. I am very gratified. I-- I see the results of my suffering, so it makes the pain easier when I see the children being so receptive to what I have to say. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] All right. I am sitting here in my living room at 443 Terhune Avenue, in Passaic, New Jersey. With me are my husband, my beloved husband, and my precious son, whom I love dearly. I am very happy to be alive and sit in our house by my children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my son's name is Larry Bachner. And, of course, this is my husband, Max Bachner. And-- and I'm his wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: My son-- my son is an attorney, I can proudly say. And I'm very happy for a job well done. My Michael is also an attorney. He is not here, but he promised to come. Maybe he couldn't make it. I-- my daughter is a social worker. I truly have Yiddish nachas, just like I wish all my good friends to enjoy the nachas. My grandchildren go to yeshivas; my daughter-in-laws are nice, beautiful people. And God should grant us health, happiness, and above all, peace on earth for all mankind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does anyone else have anything they'd like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd-- I personally thank God for meeting my wife, to-- well, to be so lucky to have a wife like I have. And thank God for the grandchildren, for my children. I was a lucky person. And I am thanking God for that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I want to say how proud I am of my parents. I love them very much. I also thank God that they were of the fortunate few to survive. And I couldn't have been blessed with better parents.
